title: Biological Agents & Pathogens
description: Questions about the organisms, agents, and biological methods in your research.

questions.text: What containment level is required for the organisms or biological agents in your research?
questions.helpText: ACDP Hazard Groups determine the required Containment Level (CL). CL1 = low risk, CL2 = moderate, CL3 = serious disease risk, CL4 = highest risk. Check the ACDP Approved List: https://www.hse.gov.uk/pUbns/misc/energen.htm

options.label: Containment Level 1 / Hazard Group 1 (unlikely to cause human disease)
Containment Level 2 / Hazard Group 2 (can cause disease; treatment available)
Containment Level 3 / Hazard Group 3 (serious disease; may spread)
Containment Level 4 / Hazard Group 4 (severe disease; no treatment)
I'm not sure of the containment level

signals.concernDescription: Your research involves an ACDP Hazard Group 3 organism, which can cause severe human disease. This requires Containment Level 3 facilities and carries dual-use considerations.
Your research involves an ACDP Hazard Group 4 organism — the highest risk category. This requires maximum containment and carries significant dual-use and biosecurity implications.
You were unsure of the hazard group classification. Consider checking the ACDP Approved List of Biological Agents or consulting your institutional biosafety officer.

questions.text: Are any of the organisms in your research on the Australia Group list of biological agents?
questions.helpText: The Australia Group maintains a list of biological agents with potential for use in biological weapons. This includes many common research organisms. See: https://www.dfat.gov.au/publications/minisite/theaustraliagroupnet/site/en/human_animal_pathogens.html

signals.concernDescription: Your research involves an organism on the Australia Group biological agents list. This carries export control and additional oversight considerations.
You were unsure whether your organisms appear on the Australia Group list. Consider checking the list or consulting your institution's export control officer.

questions.text: Does your research involve any of the following?
questions.helpText: These are internationally recognised categories of gain-of-function research with dual-use potential, drawn from the US DURC policy and the Canadian PHAC framework.

options.label: Enhancing resistance to therapeutics or vaccines
Rendering a population more susceptible to an agent
Generating or reconstituting an eradicated or extinct agent
Enhancing environmental stability or decontamination resistance
Enhancing capacity to interfere with diagnostics or surveillance

signals.concernDescription: Your research involves gain-of-function work that falls within internationally recognised categories of dual-use concern.
Generating or reconstituting an eradicated or extinct agent is among the most serious dual-use concerns. This work requires specialist oversight and robust justification.

questions.text: Could the methods or techniques you are developing be applied to organisms other than your study organism — including organisms that could be weaponised?

signals.concernDescription: Your methods could be applied to organisms beyond your study system, including organisms with higher biosecurity implications.
You were unsure whether your methods could be applied to higher-risk organisms. Consider discussing this with a biosecurity specialist.

questions.text: Does your research produce detailed protocols that could enable someone without specialist training to work with dangerous biological agents?

signals.concernDescription: Your work generates protocols that could enable non-specialist work with dangerous agents. This is an information hazard that requires careful management of publication and sharing.
You were unsure whether your protocols could enable non-specialist work with dangerous agents.
